            # DAG Walker Directive

## Strategy context
The genus of a smooth proper curve (`def:genus`, consumed by the project spine)
is defined through the Riemann–Roch machinery, which rests on a large
sheaf-cohomology infrastructure: Čech cohomology with k-module coefficients, the
Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence, the structure sheaf as a sheaf of k-modules
/ abelian groups, higher direct images, and flat base change. This is the
LARGEST disconnected region of the blueprint: ~120 isolated nodes across the
Cohomology and RiemannRoch chapters. The mathematics and proofs are already
written in these chapters; what is missing is the `\uses{}` wiring tying them
into one cone under the RR genus head.

## Depth / scope
**Your write domain is ONLY the chapters matching `Cohomology_*.tex` and
`RiemannRoch_*.tex`.** Three parallel walkers own the other regions — do NOT
edit Jacobian/AbelJacobi/Genus, Rigidity, Picard, or Albanese chapters.

Walk UP from `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` and from the other RR heads
(`thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`, and the RR.* / H1-vanishing /
WeilDivisor / OCofP / OcOfD / RationalCurveIso top declarations) and make the
whole Cohomology+RiemannRoch cone complete:

1. For each isolated node in your chapters (use
   `archon dag-query ancestors --node thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero --json` and
   `leandag show isolated --json` filtered to your chapters), read its statement
   and proof and add the `\uses{}` edges its proof actually invokes — both
   intra-chapter and to other Cohomology/RiemannRoch chapters you own.
2. The big internal clusters to wire: the Mayer–Vietoris long-exact-sequence
   family (`Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex`, ~56 nodes), the sheaf-of-k-modules /
   sheafification / Ext / structure-sheaf family
   (`Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`, ~46 nodes), structure sheaf as
   abelian groups (`Cohomology_StructureSheafAb.tex`), higher direct images and
   flat base change, and the RR formula chain
   (Euler characteristic → skyscraper H^0/H^1 → RR formula → genus-zero RR).
3. Connect the RiemannRoch layer DOWN to the Cohomology layer: RR statements
   about H^0/H^1 dimensions `\uses` the cohomology definitions that compute
   them.
4. Where the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib provides as-is (e.g. snake
   lemma / long exact sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes,
   sheafification existence, Ext functoriality), and there is NOT already a
   project block proving it, you MAY author a Mathlib dependency anchor
   (statement + `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` + `\mathlibok` + `\textit{Provided
   by Mathlib.}`) — but ONLY if you can name the genuine Mathlib declaration;
   otherwise leave it and report it. Many of these nodes are already
   "proved directly in Lean" (effort 0) — for those just add the `\uses{}`
   edges; do not touch their proof state.

**Boundary rule:** `def:genus` (Genus.tex) is owned by the Backbone walker — it
will add the edge from `def:genus` down to your RR head. You do NOT edit
Genus.tex. Stop at the top of your region; record the handoff.

For any node in your chapters with a real statement but no `\lean{}` and not a
`\begin{remark}`, add a placeholder `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.<name>}`.

Do NOT add `\leanok`. Do NOT invent new mathematics — the proofs are already
written; you are transcribing dependencies. If a node genuinely has no proof and
no Lean (∞), report it under "Could not complete"; do not fabricate.

## References
- `references/stacks-coherent.md` (Stacks ch.30, tag 02KH flat base change of

- `references/stacks-varieties.md` (tag 0BUG, H^0(X,O) lemma) — if you cite the
  global-sections / structure-sheaf facts.
  Cite verbatim from the local files only; do not quote from memory.

2	name: dag-walker
3	description: Walk UP a target declaration's dependency cone in the leandag graph and make it complete — every dependency has a statement, an informal proof (finite effort, not ∞), and a complete \uses{} set. Turns ∞-effort cones into finite ones and makes the frontier trustworthy. Plan/dag-agent-dispatched against a seed node.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made
10	    complete: an ∞-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a
11	    frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\uses{}` list. Name the seed
12	    label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.
13	  - I am the "is the foundation under this target complete?" agent —
14	    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise
15	    direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across
16	    chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write
17	    domain when the cone spans chapters:
18	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'
19	    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a
20	    source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).
21	  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't
22	    trust: a node looks ready when its `\uses{}` under-declares its real
23	    dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.
24	  - My report's "Could not complete" section lists dependencies that are
25	    genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy
26	    items — read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.
27	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** — that is the deterministic
28	    sync_leanok phase's job. `\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine
29	    Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).
30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.
113	
114	## Logging
115	
116	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
117	
118	```markdown
119	# DAG Walker Report
120	
121	## Slug
122	<slug>
123	
124	## Seed
125	<label>
126	
127	## Status
128	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
129	
130	## Cone before → after
131	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
132	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
133	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
134	
135	## Blocks added / proofs written
136	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
137	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
138	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
139	
140	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
141	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
142	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
143	
144	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
145	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
146	
147	## References consulted
148	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
149	
150	## Notes for dispatcher
151	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
152	```
153	
154	## Return Value
155	
156	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.
157

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

363	\begin{lemma}\leanok
364	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
365	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
367	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
368	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
369	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
370	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
371	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
372	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
373	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
374	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
375	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
376	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
377	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
378	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
379	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
380	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
381	  \[
382	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
383	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
384	  \]
385	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
386	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
387	  \[
388	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
389	  \]
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
394	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
395	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
396	  \leanok
397	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
398	  (REF),
399	  \[
400	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
401	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
402	  \]
403	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
404	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
405	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
406	  by the prover.
407	
408	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
409	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
410	  \[
411	    H^0(C, k(P))
412	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
413	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
414	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
415	      k(P)
416	    \bigr)
417	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
418	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
419	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
420	    (k(P))(C),
421	  \]
422	  realised by the project lemmas
423	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
424	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
425	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
426	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
427	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
428	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
429	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
430	  goal to
431	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
432	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
433	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
434	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
435	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
436	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
437	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
438	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
439	
440	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
441	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
442	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
443	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
444	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
445	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
446	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
447	
448	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
449	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
450	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
451	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
452	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
453	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
454	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
455	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
456	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
457	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
458	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
459	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
460	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
461	  % gap to clear.
462	
463	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
464	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
465	\end{proof}

467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}

641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\section{Out of scope}
732	
733	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
734	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
735	
736	\begin{itemize}
737	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
738	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
739	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
740	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
741	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
742	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
743	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
744	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
746	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
747	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
748	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
749	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
750	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
751	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
752	    of REF the dependency on
753	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
754	    exact sequence of the closed point.
755	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
756	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
757	    premise by REF; its closure is
758	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
759	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
760	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
761	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
762	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
763	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
764	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
765	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
766	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
767	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
768	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
769	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
770	    principal-divisor identity REF from
771	    \texttt{RR.1}.
772	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
773	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
774	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
775	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
776	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
777	    curve specialisation.
778	\end{itemize}

# DAG Walker Directive

## Slug
cohom-mv

## Seed
thm:Scheme_subsingleton_HModule_prime_supr_of_hasCechToHModuleIso_curve

## Strategy context
You are a child of the `cohom-rr` dag-walker, which is wiring the whole
Cohomology + RiemannRoch region into one dependency cone under the
Riemann--Roch genus-zero head. The Mayer--Vietoris chapter is the largest
single unwired cluster (~56 isolated nodes): it builds the Mayer--Vietoris
short-exact-sequence / long-exact-sequence machinery for the project's
`ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf cohomology `HModule`/`HModule_prime`, and the
Čech-to-HModule comparison that proves `H^1` vanishing on a curve.

## Depth / scope
**Your write domain is ONLY `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex`.**
Do NOT edit any other chapter. You MAY (and should) add `\uses{}` edges that
point at labels living in OTHER chapters you do not edit — in particular the
`def:Scheme_HModule*`, `def:Scheme_cech*`, `def:Scheme_IsCechAcyclicCover`,
`thm:Scheme_*` declarations in `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex` (a sibling
walker is wiring that file in parallel; the labels already exist on disk).

### CRITICAL MECHANICAL FACT (verified this iteration)
The leandag graph builder reads `\uses{...}` **only from the statement block**
(the `\begin{lemma/theorem/definition/...}` ... environment that carries the
`\label{}`), **NOT from the `\begin{proof}` block.** Many nodes here already
have a `\uses{}` inside their proof but show as isolated (`dep=0`) because the
statement block has none. Therefore:
- For every node, ensure the **statement block** (right after its `\label{}` /
  `\lean{}`) carries a `\uses{...}` listing every dependency its statement and
  proof actually invoke.
- If a `\uses{}` already exists only in the proof, copy it up to the statement
  (you may leave the proof copy in place; it is harmless).
- Read each block's statement + proof prose and add the edges the mathematics
  actually uses. The prose frequently names dependencies in `\texttt{...}` or
  with `\cref{...}`, and sometimes with the literal placeholder `REF` where a
  cross-reference was stripped — resolve those to the real `\label`.

### Your job
Walk every isolated node in `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` and give its
statement block a complete `\uses{}`. The internal structure is a linear-ish
build: `toBiprod`/`fromBiprod` → `shortComplex` (f mono, g epi, exact,
shortExact) → `delta`/`sequence` → `sequenceIso` → `sequence_exact` →
`AffineCoverMVSquare` family → `finrank`/`module_finite` transfer →
`subsingleton_HModule(_prime)` vanishing → `cechToHModuleIso` /
`HasAffineCechAcyclicCover` heads. Wire each node to the prior nodes (and to the
`StructureSheafModuleK` defs) its proof relies on. `_curve` variants `\uses`
their non-`_curve` counterpart plus the curve hypotheses.

After editing, re-run
`archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` on several heads to confirm
`dep_count > 0`, and
`archon dag-query ancestors --node thm:Scheme_subsingleton_HModule_prime_supr_of_hasCechToHModuleIso_curve --json`
should return a non-empty cone.

## Constraints
- Do NOT add `\leanok`. Do NOT add `\mathlibok` unless a node genuinely
  re-exports a real named Mathlib declaration (most here are proved directly in
  Lean — just add `\uses{}`, do not touch their proof state).
- Do NOT invent mathematics or rewrite proofs. You are transcribing
  dependencies only.
- Keep valid LaTeX (balanced braces in `\uses{}`).
- Any node with a real statement but no `\lean{}` and not a `\begin{remark}`:
  add a placeholder `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.<name>}`.

## References
None needed — these are project-internal constructions; do not cite external
sources unless a block already carries a `% SOURCE` comment you are extending.

## Report
Write to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-cohom-mv.md` per the standard
DAG-walker report format.

17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
