136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\section{Immediate corollaries}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
401	
402	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
403	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
404	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
405	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\( is exact on \)\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
406	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
407	  \[
408	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
409	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
410	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
411	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
413	  \]
414	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
415	  \begin{itemize}
416	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
417	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
418	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
419	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
420	      ring-theoretic identity
421	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
422	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
423	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
424	      in \(D\));
425	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
426	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
427	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
428	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
429	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
430	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
431	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
432	      \cong k(P)\( is just \)k(P)\(; away from \)P$ the skyscraper is the
433	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
434	  \end{itemize}
435	  Substituting yields
436	  \[
437	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
438	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
439	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
441	  \]
442	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
443	
444	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
445	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
446	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
447	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
448	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
449	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
450	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
451	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
452	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
453	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
454	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
455	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
457	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
458	\end{proof}
459	
460	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
461	
462	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
463	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
464	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
465	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
466	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
467	
468	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
469	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
470	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
471	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
472	to a well-defined inclusion
473	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
474	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
475	strict functoriality holds.
476	
477	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
478	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
479	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
480	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
481	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
482	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
483	
484	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
485	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
486	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
487	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
488	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
489	to two ingredients:
490	\begin{enumerate}
491	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
492	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
493	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
494	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
495	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
496	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
497	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
498	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
499	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
500	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
501	    prime divisor.
502	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
503	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
504	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
505	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
506	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
507	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
508	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
509	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
510	\end{enumerate}
511	
512	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
513	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
514	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
515	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
516	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
517	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
518	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
519	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
520	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
521	-n_Q\( iff \)\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
522	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
523	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
524	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
525	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
526	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
527	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
528	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
529	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
530	
531	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
532	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
533	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
534	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
535	landed the analogous bottom-up
536	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
537	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
538	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
539	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).
540

580	\section{Out of scope}
581	
582	\begin{itemize}
583	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
584	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
585	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
586	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
587	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
588	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
589	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
590	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
591	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
592	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
593	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
594	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
595	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
596	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
597	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
598	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
599	    REF at the single instance
600	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
601	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
602	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
603	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
604	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
605	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
606	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
607	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
608	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
609	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
610	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
611	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
612	    space.} The identification
613	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
614	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
615	    REF for general
616	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
617	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
618	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
619	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
620	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
621	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
622	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
623	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
624	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
625	    does not engage them.
626	\end{itemize}

441	\begin{lemma}
442	
443	
444	
445	\leanok
446	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
447	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
449	  
450	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
451	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
452	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
453	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
455	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
456	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
457	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
458	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
459	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
460	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
461	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
462	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
463	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
464	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
465	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
466	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
467	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
468	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
469	  \[
470	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
471	  \]
472	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  
477	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
478	  exact sequence
479	  \[
480	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
481	  \]
482	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
483	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
484	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
485	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
486	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
487	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
488	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
489	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
490	  \[
491	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
492	  \]
493	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
494	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
495	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
496	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
497	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
498	  \begin{multline*}
499	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
500	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
501	  \end{multline*}
502	  Two ingredients close the proof:
503	
504	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
505	  hypothesis: by REF,
506	  \[
507	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
508	  \]
509	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
510	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
511	
512	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
513	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
514	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
515	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
516	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
517	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
518	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
519	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
520	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
521	
522	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
523	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
524	  \[
525	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
526	  \]
527	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
528	
529	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
530	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
531	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
532	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
533	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
534	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
535	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
536	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
537	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
538	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
539	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
540	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
541	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
542	  complex degenerates).
543	\end{proof}

545	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
546

555	\begin{theorem}
556	
557	
558	
559	\leanok
560	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
561	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
563	  
564	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
565	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
566	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
567	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
568	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
569	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
570	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
571	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
573	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
574	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
575	  % is the Euler characteristic
576	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
577	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
578	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
579	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
580	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
581	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
582	  \[
583	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
584	  \]
585	\end{theorem}
586	
587	\begin{proof}
588	  
589	  
590	  
591	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
592	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
593	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
594	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
595	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
596	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
597	  \[
598	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
599	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
600	  \]
601	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
602	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
603	  \[
604	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
605	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
606	    \;=\; 2.
607	  \]
608	  By
609	  REF, the second
610	  term vanishes:
611	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
612	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
613	
614	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
615	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
616	  REF computes
617	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
618	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
619	  \[
620	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
621	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
622	  \]
623	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
624	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
625	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
626	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
627	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
628	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
629	  segment yields the short exact sequence
630	  \[
631	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
632	  \]
633	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
634	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
635	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
636	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
637	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
638	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
639	\end{proof}

920	\begin{corollary}
921	
922	
923	
924	\leanok
925	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
926	single simple pole]
927	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
928	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
929	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
930	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
931	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
932	  
933	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
934	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
935	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
936	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
937	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
938	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
939	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
940	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
941	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
942	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
943	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
944	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
945	  \begin{itemize}
946	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
947	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
948	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
949	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
950	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
951	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
952	  \end{itemize}
953	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
954	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
955	\end{corollary}
956	
957	\begin{proof}
958	  
959	  
960	  
961	  By
962	  REF,
963	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
964	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
965	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
966	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
967	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
968	  identification of
969	  REF), is a one-dimensional
970	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
971	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
972	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
973	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
974	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
975	
976	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
977	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
978	  REF the section
979	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
980	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
981	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
982	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
983	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
984	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
985	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
986	  of the statement are now immediate.
987	
988	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
989	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
990	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
991	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
992	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
993	
994	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
995	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
996	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
997	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
998	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
999	  REF's output. The
1000	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
1001	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
1002	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
1003	\end{proof}

493	\begin{lemma}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
497	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
499	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
500	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
501	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
502	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
503	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
504	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
505	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
506	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
507	  % X\) is the inclusion."
508	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
509	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
510	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
511	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
512	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
513	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is isomorphic to the
514	  pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
515	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
516	
517	  % NOTE (iter-197 lean-vs-blueprint-checker M-2): The Lean signature
518	  % `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` (file
519	  % `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean`, L818) is
520	  % materially weaker than the mathematical statement displayed above:
521	  % the Lean conclusion is
522	  %     `Nonempty (skyscraperSheaf P A ≅
523	  %        (pushforward _ _).obj ((constantSheaf _ _).obj A))`,
524	  % i.e.\ the existence of an isomorphism rather than a concrete one.
525	  % The `Nonempty` wrapper is forced by the use of `Classical.choice`
526	  % to unpack the inner iso of
527	  % \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}, which itself
528	  % carries a typed sorry pending the Mathlib gap discussed there.
529	  % When that inner iso is constructed explicitly, the `Nonempty`
530	  % wrapper can be removed and the present declaration upgraded to
531	  % a direct concrete-iso statement (no consumer change required at
532	  % the headline `H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` because it
533	  % does not extract iso morphisms from this lemma).
534	\end{lemma}
535	
536	\begin{proof}
537	  \uses{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
538	  \leanok
539	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
540	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
541	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
542	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
543	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
544	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
545	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
546	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
547	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
548	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
549	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
550	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
551	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
552	
553	  In the Lean formalisation this argument is split into two pieces.
554	  The \emph{outer} step is the presheaf-level equality
555	  \[
556	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A \;=\;
557	      \bigl(i_*\;\bigr)
558	      \bigl(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  obtained from Mathlib's \texttt{skyscraperPresheaf\_eq\_pushforward}
561	  via \texttt{ObjectProperty.FullSubcategory.ext} (axiom-clean, no
562	  Mathlib gap). The \emph{inner} step is the iso
563	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A \cong
564	    (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit})\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A\)
565	  on the one-point space \(\PUnit\); this is the actual blocking
566	  technical step and is isolated as
567	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit} below. Composing
568	  the two pieces (with the inner iso unpacked via
569	  \texttt{Classical.choice} from its current \(\mathtt{Nonempty}\)
570	  wrapper) gives the lemma.
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{lemma}\leanok
574	[Inner iso: skyscraperSheaf vs.\ constantSheaf on $\PUnit$]
575	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
577	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
578	  \textit{Project-bespoke sub-lemma} (Lean-substrate technical step
579	  for \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}; no separate external
580	  source — the underlying mathematical content is the one-point case
581	  of Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.17 quoted in
582	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}).
583	
584	  Let \(\PUnit\) denote the one-point topological space, let
585	  \(J_{\PUnit}\) denote the Grothendieck topology of opens on
586	  \(\PUnit\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module. Then there is an
587	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(\PUnit\)
588	  \[
589	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A \;\cong\;
590	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k)\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A.
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	
594	\begin{proof}
595	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
596	  \leanok
597	  The space \(\PUnit\) has exactly two opens, \(\bot\) (empty) and
598	  \(\top\) (the whole space, which contains \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\)).
599	  We build the iso pointwise on these two opens and check naturality
600	  on the unique non-identity inclusion \(\bot \le \top\).
601	
602	  \emph{Forward map.} Apply Mathlib's
603	  \(\mathtt{constantSheafAdj}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k\;
604	   h_{T\top}\,.\,\mathtt{homEquiv}\,.\,\mathtt{symm}\) to the natural
605	  identification
606	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A\,.\,\mathrm{val}\,.
607	    \,\mathrm{obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;\top) = A\),
608	  which holds by \(\mathtt{simp}\;[\mathtt{skyscraperPresheaf}]\) since
609	  \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit} \in \top\).
610	
611	  \emph{Inverse map.} Build pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\):
612	  \begin{itemize}
613	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\bot\): both sheaves give the terminal
614	      object in \(\Module \bar k\) (the constant sheaf is the
615	      sheafification of the constant presheaf, whose value at the
616	      empty open is the zero module after sheafification; the
617	      skyscraper sheaf at the empty open is by construction
618	      \(0\)). Use \(\mathtt{IsTerminal.uniqueUpToIso}\) to identify
619	      them;
620	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\): use the constantSheaf-unit-iso on
621	      \(\PUnit\), which is true since the \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\)
622	      functor is fully faithful on \(\PUnit\) (this fully-faithful
623	      instance is the Mathlib gap described below in the route
624	      choice).
625	  \end{itemize}
626	
627	  \emph{Naturality} on the unique non-identity inclusion
628	  \(\bot \le \top\): both sides land in the terminal object at
629	  \(\bot\), so commutativity is automatic by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton}\)
630	  of the terminal object.
631	
632	  \paragraph{Mathlib gap (route choice).} The inverse map's
633	  \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\) component needs that the
634	  \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\) functor on \(\PUnit\) is fully faithful.
635	  Two routes close the gap; both are tracked for the iter-197+
636	  prover:
637	  \begin{itemize}
638	    \item \emph{Route A} (preferred — Mathlib upstreaming
639	      candidate): provide instances
640	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
641	      \(.\) \texttt{Full} and
642	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
643	      \(.\) \texttt{Faithful}. Likely the smallest project-side
644	      change once a Mathlib PR for the analogous fact for the
645	      one-point space is accepted; the inverse map then follows by
646	      \texttt{Functor.preimage} of the forward map.
647	    \item \emph{Route B} (fallback — no Mathlib upstream): directly
648	      construct the iso pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\) as
649	      above, using \(\mathtt{Sheaf.IsTerminal}\) at \(\bot\) and an
650	      explicit value extraction at \(\top\) (the constant sheaf at
651	      the unique non-empty open of an irreducible space is the
652	      identity on \(A\) after sheafification — same fact as
653	      \cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible} for the non-empty
654	      branch, used here only at the one-point space). Estimated
655	      LOC: ~50-80 standalone.
656	  \end{itemize}
657	\end{proof}

659	\begin{lemma}
660	
661	\leanok
662	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
663	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
665	
666	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
667	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
668	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
669	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
670	
671	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
672	  no external source pinned).
673	\end{lemma}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	
677	
678	\leanok
679	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
680	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
681	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
682	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
683	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}
687	
688	\leanok
689	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
690	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
691	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
692	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
693	
694	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
695	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
696	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
697	  \[
698	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
699	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
700	  \]
701	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
702	
703	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
704	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
705	  above).
706	\end{lemma}
707	
708	\begin{proof}

81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	
84	\leanok
85	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
86	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
87	  
88	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
89	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
90	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
91	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
92	  \[
93	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
94	  \]
95	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
96	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
97	  \[
98	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
99	  \]
100	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
101	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
102	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
103	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
104	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
106	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
107	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
108	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	\begin{proof}
111	
112	
113	
114	\leanok
115	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
116	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
117	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
118	  under the name
119	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
120	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
121	  \begin{quote}
122	  \small
123	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
124	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
125	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
127	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
128	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
129	  \end{quote}
130	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
131	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
132	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
133	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
134	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
135	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
136	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
137	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
138	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
139	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
140	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
141	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
142	  project-bespoke wrapper
143	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
144	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
145	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
146	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
147	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
148	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
149	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
150	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
151	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
152	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
153	\end{proof}

162	\begin{lemma}
163	
164	
165	\leanok
166	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
167	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}

170	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
171	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
172	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).

321	\begin{lemma}
322	
323	
324	\leanok
325	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
326	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
327	  
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
329	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
330	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
331	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
332	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
333	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
334	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
335	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
336	  % equivalent:}
337	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
338	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
339	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
341	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
342	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
343	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
344	  (p.~299).}
345	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
346	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
347	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
348	  degree one,
349	  \[
350	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
351	  \]
352	  formally
353	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
354	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
355	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
356	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
357	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
358	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
359	  \[
360	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
361	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
362	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
363	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
364	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
365	  \]
366	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
367	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
368	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
369	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
370	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
371	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
372	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
373	\end{lemma}
374	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
375	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
376	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
377	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
378	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
379	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
380	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
381	\begin{proof}
382	
383	
384	
385	\leanok
386	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
387	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
388	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
389	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
390	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
391	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
392	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
393	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
394	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
395	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
396	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
397	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
398	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
399	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
400	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
401	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
402	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
403	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
404	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
405	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
406	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
407	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
408	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
409	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
410	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
411	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
412	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
413	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
414	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
415	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
416	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
417	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
418	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
419	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
420	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
421	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
422	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
423	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
424	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
425	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
426	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
427	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
428	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
429	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
430	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
431	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
432	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
433	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
434	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
435	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
436	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
437	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
438	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
439	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
440	\end{proof}

669	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
670	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
671	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
672	  
673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
674	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
675	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
676	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
677	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
678	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
679	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
680	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
681	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
682	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
683	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
684	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
685	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
686	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
687	  \[
688	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
689	  \]
690	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
691	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
692	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
693	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
694	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
695	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
696	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
697	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
698	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
699	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
700	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
701	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
702	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
703	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
704	  codim-\(1\) regime:
705	  \[
706	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
707	      \begin{cases}
708	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
709	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
710	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
711	      \end{cases}
712	  \]
713	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
714	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
715	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
716	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
717	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
718	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
719	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
720	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
721	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
722	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
723	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
724	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
725	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
726	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
727	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
728	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
729	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
730	\end{definition}

774	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
775	\begin{definition}
776	\leanok
777	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
778	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
779	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
780	  
781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
782	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
783	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
784	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
785	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
786	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
787	  nonsingular curve).}
788	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
789	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
790	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
791	  \[
792	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
793	  \]
794	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
795	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
796	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
797	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
798	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
799	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
800	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
801	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
802	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
803	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
804	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
805	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
806	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
807	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
808	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
809	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
810	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
811	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
812	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
813	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
814	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
815	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
816	\end{definition}
817	\begin{theorem}
818	\leanok
819	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
820	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
821	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
822	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
823	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
824	  homomorphism
825	  \[
826	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
827	    \qquad
828	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
829	    \qquad
830	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
831	  \]
832	\end{theorem}
833	\begin{proof}
834	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
835	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
836	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
837	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
838	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
839	\end{proof}
840	\section{Principal divisors}
841	\begin{lemma}
842	
843	\leanok
844	[Finite support of the order function — Hartshorne 6.1 / Stacks 02RV]
845	  \label{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_order_finite_support}
847	  % \lean{...} corrected iter-199 review: the iter-199 plan-phase
848	  % pin used the wrong namespace (".Scheme.") and the Lean theorem at
849	  % WeilDivisor.lean:357 sits in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` only
850	  % (Scheme.RationalMap closed at L159). The theorem is also declared
851	  % `private` — sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. The
852	  % chapter pin is retained as documentation for the future-closed
853	  % version; the iter-199 plan agent's directive to consume this via
854	  % the `[IsNoetherian X]` strengthening remains the closure path.
855	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:prime_divisor}
856	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (read from
857	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
858	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
859	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
860	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
861	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (a.k.a.\ Stacks 02RV).}
862	
863	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral scheme that is regular in
864	  codimension one, and let \(f \in K(X)\) be a nonzero rational function.
865	  Then \(\{Y \in X^{(1)} \mid \ord_Y(f) \ne 0\}\) is finite.
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open
869	% affine subset of \(X\) on which \(f\) is regular, \(f \in A\). Then for any
870	% prime divisor \(Y\) which meets \(U\), \(Y \cap U\) corresponds to a minimal
871	% prime ideal \(\mathfrak p\) of \(A\) containing \(f\). But these are finite
872	% in number (II, 3.10). On the other hand, \(X - U\) is a proper closed
873	% subset of \(X\), so it can contain only finitely many prime divisors of
874	% \(X\) [\dots]"
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:order_at_point}
877	  Following Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV:
878	  \begin{enumerate}
879	  \item Pick a nonempty affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R \subset X\) on
880	    which \(f\) is regular. Then \(R\) is a Noetherian integral domain.
881	  \item For each prime divisor \(Y\) meeting \(U\), the corresponding
882	    prime \(\mathfrak p_Y\) is a height-1 prime of \(R\); the height-1 primes
883	    containing \(f \cdot 1\) are precisely the minimal primes of
884	    \((f)\), which by Krull's Hauptidealsatz form a finite set (Stacks tag
885	    00KZ / Atiyah--Macdonald 11.16). Of those, \(\ord_Y(f) \ne 0\) holds
886	    only at the finitely many minimal primes of \((f)\) over \(R\).
887	  \item The remaining prime divisors \(Y\) lie in the closed complement
888	    \(X \setminus U\), which is a proper closed subset of \(X\); the
889	    Noetherian topological structure of \(X\) (Stacks 04ZE) bounds the
890	    irreducible components of \(X \setminus U\) of codimension one in
891	    \(X\) by a finite number. For each such component, the stalk
892	    \(\mathcal O_{X, Y}\) is a DVR (regularity in codim 1) and \(f\) has
893	    finite valuation; together these contribute only finitely many
894	    nonzero values to the order function.
895	  \item Combining (2) and (3) gives the finiteness of the support.
896	  \end{enumerate}
897	  \emph{Hypothesis health.} The argument in (3) uses that \(X\) is
898	  Noetherian as a topological space (so \(X \setminus U\) has finitely
899	  many irreducible components). For \([X]\) only locally Noetherian +
900	  integral (without quasi-compactness), one can produce
901	  counter-examples with infinitely many disjoint codim-1 prime
902	  divisors outside \(U\). The Lean signature thus takes
903	  \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]} (or equivalently
904	  \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]}). The hybrid
905	  WeilDivisor file pins the public-facing declaration under the
906	  weaker \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]} hypothesis and exposes the
907	  \texttt{[CompactSpace X]} (or full Noetherian) strengthening
908	  as the substantive sub-claim that this lemma resolves.
909	\end{proof}
