911	\begin{definition}
912	\leanok
913	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point, lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
914	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
915	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
916	  
917	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
918	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
919	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
920	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
921	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
922	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
923	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
924	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
925	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
926	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
927	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
928	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
929	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
930	  p.~131.}
931	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
932	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
933	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
934	  \[
935	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
936	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
937	  \]
938	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
939	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
940	  the same definition specialises to
941	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
942	  sum over closed points.
943	\end{definition}
944	\begin{theorem}
945	\leanok
946	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
947	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
948	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
949	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
950	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
951	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
952	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
953	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
954	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
955	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
956	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
957	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
958	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
959	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
960	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
961	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
962	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
963	  \[
964	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
965	    \qquad
966	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
967	    \qquad
968	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
969	  \]
970	\end{theorem}
971	\begin{proof}
972	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
973	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
974	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
975	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
976	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
977	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
978	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
979	  identities.
980	\end{proof}
981	\begin{theorem}
982	\leanok
983	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
984	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
985	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
986	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
987	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
988	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
989	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
990	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
991	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
992	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
993	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
994	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
995	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
996	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
997	  \[
998	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
999	  \]
1000	\end{theorem}
1001	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
1002	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
1003	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
1004	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
1005	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
1006	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
1007	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
1008	\begin{proof}
1009	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
1010	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
1011	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
1012	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
1013	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
1014	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
1015	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
1016	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
1017	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
1018	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
1019	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
1020	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
1021	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
1022	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
1023	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
1024	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
1025	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
1026	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
1027	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
1028	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
1029	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
1030	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
1031	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
1032	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
1033	\end{proof}
1034	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
1035	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
1036	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`

1042	\begin{definition}
1043	
1044	
1045	\leanok
1046	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
1047	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
1049	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
1050	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
1051	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
1052	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
1053	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
1054	  \[
1055	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
1056	  \]
1057	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
1058	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
1059	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
1060	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
1061	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
1062	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
1063	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
1064	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
1065	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
1066	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
1067	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
1068	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
1069	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
1070	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
1071	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
1072	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
1073	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
1074	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	\begin{lemma}
1077	
1078	\leanok
1079	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
1080	function equals the function-field extension degree]
1081	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
1082	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
1083	  % NOTE (iter-194 refactor v2 `lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2`):
1084	  % the iter-193 `hlp`-augmented signature was diagnosed STILL false by
1085	  % the Lane I prover via counter-witness `K = K(C) = \bar k(u),
1086	  % t = u(u-1)`. Iter-194 plan-phase refactor reshapes the Lean
1087	  % signature per Option (b): drop the abstract `K` parameter and pin
1088	  % `t \in K(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = (\text{ProjectiveLineBar
1089	  % kbar}).left.functionField`; replace `hlp` with the stronger
1090	  % uniformiser hypothesis
1091	  %   `hLPUnif : \exists Y_0 \in \text{PrimeDivisor}(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}),
1092	  %     order(Y_0, t) = 1 \land
1093	  %     \forall Y, order(Y, t) > 0 \implies Y = Y_0`,
1094	  % i.e. \(t\) has a unique simple zero on \(\mathbb P^1\). This rules
1095	  % out the iter-193 counter-witness because (i) \(t = u(u-1)\) lives
1096	  % in \(K(\mathbb P^1)\) but has TWO simple zeros (\(\{u = 0\}\),
1097	  % \(\{u = 1\}\)), so the uniqueness clause fails; (ii) any
1098	  % function-field embedding `K = K(C)` is now disallowed at the type
1099	  % level. The consumer
1100	  % `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
1101	  % (`RationalCurveIso.lean`) supplies the witness via
1102	  % `localParameterAtInfty kbar = 1/u` (a Möbius transformation; simple
1103	  % zero only at \(\infty\)) as a typed sorry owed iter-194+. Refactor
1104	  % v2 net cost: \(+2\) typed-sorry typeclass instances on
1105	  % `ProjectiveLineBar` (\verb|IsLocallyNoetherian|,
1106	  % \verb|IsRegularInCodimensionOne|; both directly derivable from
1107	  % `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` ⟹ regular ⟹ Noetherian + DVR-stalks).
1108	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
1109	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
1110	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
1111	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
1112	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
1113	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
1114	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
1115	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
1116	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
1117	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
1118	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1119	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
1120	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
1121	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
1122	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
1123	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
1124	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
1125	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
1126	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
1127	  extension degree:
1128	  \[
1129	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
1130	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
1131	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1132	  \]
1133	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
1134	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
1135	\end{lemma}
1136	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
1137	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
1138	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
1139	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
1140	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
1141	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
1142	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
1143	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
1144	\begin{proof}
1145	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
1146	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
1147	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
1148	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
1149	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
1150	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
1151	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
1152	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
1153	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
1154	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
1155	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
1156	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
1157	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
1158	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
1159	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
1160	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
1161	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
1162	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
1163	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
1164	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
1165	  \[
1166	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
1167	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
1168	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
1169	  \]
1170	  where the second equality uses
1171	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
1172	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
1173	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
1174	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
1175	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
1176	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
1177	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
1178	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
1179	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
1180	  \[
1181	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
1182	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1183	  \]
1184	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
1185	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
1186	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
1187	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
1188	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
1189	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
1190	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
1191	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
1192	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
1193	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
1194	  Therefore
1195	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
1196	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
1197	  taking degree completes the proof.
1198	\end{proof}
1199	\section{Divisor class group}
1200	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
1201	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
1202	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
1203	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
1204	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
1205	out of scope here.
1206	\begin{definition}
1207	\leanok
1208	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
1209	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
1210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
1211	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
1212	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
1213	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
1214	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
1215	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
1216	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
1217	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
1218	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
1219	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
1220	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
1221	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
1222	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
1223	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
1224	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
1225	  \[
1226	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
1227	  \]
1228	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
1229	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
1230	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
1231	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
1232	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
1233	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
1234	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
1235	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
1236	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
1237	\end{definition}
1238	\section{Out of scope}
1239	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
1240	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
1241	\begin{itemize}
1242	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
1243	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
1244	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
1245	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
1246	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
1247	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
1248	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
1249	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
1250	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
1251	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1252	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
1253	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
1254	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
1255	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
1256	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
1257	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
1258	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
1259	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
1260	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
1261	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
1262	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
1263	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
1264	    strictly curve-focused.
1265	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
1266	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
1267	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
1268	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
1269	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
1270	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
1271	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39
